methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: planqc
private.rationale: Same sector (Quantum Computing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Nu Quantum is a Series A quantum computing company that has raised £8.5M+ in equity alongside government contracts from the UK Ministry of Defence and DSIT. The target operates within a sector characterized by 11 total comparable companies: 3 public peers (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Systems) and 8 private firms including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti, D-Wave Quantum, and Infleqtion.

Peer-median valuation methodology was deemed inapplicable due to insufficient comparable datapoints. The quantum computing sector contains only 7 same-sector peers overall, but filtering by stage alignment and valuation tier yielded fewer than the three datapoints required for robust median calculation. No Series A quantum firms with comparable characteristics and disclosed valuations were identified, nor were adjacent-stage peers sufficient in number. Cross-sector median application would introduce excessive methodological noise given the sector's distinctive unit economics, technology risk profile, and government contracting dynamics.

Without triangulated valuation output, this analysis cannot support a specific range. Progression toward a Series B valuation benchmark would require either (i) disclosure of comparable Series A quantum raises with explicit post-money valuations, (ii) clarification of government contract terms and revenue recognition timing, or (iii) explicit founder guidance on valuation expectations. Key uncertainties include the quantum computing sector's remaining pre-commercialization phase, concentration of technical talent, and regulatory/export control constraints on UK quantum IP.
